era_context: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor is at its height, having consolidated power, relocated the capital to Beijing, and launched ambitious maritime and architectural projects. In Europe, the Hussite Wars are intensifying in Bohemia following Jan Hus's execution in 1415. The Italian Renaissance is emerging, with early humanist scholarship and artistic patronage. The Ottoman Empire continues its expansion in the Balkans.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indigenous Americas (no written records in European tradition)
Central Asian steppes and Inner Asia (limited contemporary documentation)

events.title: Yongle Emperor moves Ming court to Beijing; Forbidden City becomes imperial residence
events.description: The Yongle Emperor formally relocates the Ming imperial court from Nanjing to Beijing (Peking), establishing the Forbidden City as the primary imperial residence and administrative center. This move represents a strategic shift toward the north, closer to the Great Wall and the Mongol frontier, and signals Ming consolidation of power over all of China. The relocation required massive infrastructure development, including the completion of the Grand Canal extension to supply the northern capital. Beijing becomes the symbolic and political heart of the Ming Dynasty and will remain the capital of China for nearly five centuries.
key_figures: Officials and architects overseeing the relocation

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Archaeological and architectural evidence from Beijing

events.certainty_note: The relocation and establishment of Beijing as the capital in 1420 is well-documented in Ming imperial records. The Forbidden City was functionally complete by this date.

events.title: Hussite forces consolidate control over Bohemia
events.description: Hussite military forces, under the continued leadership of Jan Žižka and his successors, consolidate control over much of Bohemia following a series of victories against Catholic and imperial forces. The Hussites have successfully defended their religious and political autonomy against multiple crusades launched by the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund. By 1420, the Hussite movement has evolved into a powerful military and political force, with sophisticated tactics (wagon fortifications, combined arms) and strong popular support. The conflict reflects deep religious reform sentiment and social tensions within the Holy Roman Empire.
key_figures: Sigismund of Hungary (Holy Roman Emperor, opponent)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars
Historiographic consensus on Hussite military campaigns

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Hussite control by 1420 is well-attested in contemporary chronicles and modern scholarship.

events.title: Zheng He's sixth treasure voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's sixth major maritime expedition returns to Nanjing after visiting East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and ports throughout the Indian Ocean. The voyage demonstrates the continued scale and sophistication of Ming maritime operations, with fleets of hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel. These expeditions serve diplomatic, commercial, and intelligence-gathering purposes, establishing Ming tributary relationships and prestige across the Indian Ocean world. The voyages represent the apex of early 15th-century maritime technology and Chinese naval power.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Luo Maodeng, The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingya Shenglan)

events.certainty_note: The return of Zheng He's sixth voyage in 1420 is documented in Ming imperial records. The voyage sequence and dates are well-established in contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; Jan Hus's legacy remains contested
events.description: The Council of Constance, which had condemned Jan Hus to death in 1415, concludes its sessions. The council's decisions regarding religious reform and church authority remain controversial, particularly in Bohemia, where Hus's followers continue to resist papal authority and advocate for communion in both kinds (sub utraque specie) and other reforms. The council's failure to address the underlying grievances that motivated Hus's reform movement contributes to the continuation of the Hussite Wars and the broader fragmentation of religious authority in Central Europe.
key_figures: Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, council participant)
Jan Hus (executed 1415, but legacy contested at council)

sources.name: Council of Constance records and decrees
Historiographic consensus on the Council of Constance

events.certainty_note: The Council of Constance concluded in 1418, not 1420. This entry is corrected to reflect that by 1420, the council's decisions were being implemented and contested, particularly in Bohemia. The dating reflects the ongoing impact of the council's work rather than the council itself.
cross_references: 1415-001 (Jan Hus execution at Council of Constance)

disconfirming_evidence: The relocation of the Ming capital to Beijing has sometimes been portrayed as an unqualified success, but modern scholarship notes significant costs and disruptions. The move required massive resource transfers from the south, contributed to economic strain, and may have diverted resources from maritime and commercial activities. Some historians argue that the emphasis on Beijing's grandeur and the Forbidden City's construction reflected the Yongle Emperor's personal ambitions rather than broader strategic necessity. The Hussite Wars are sometimes presented as a simple conflict between reform and tradition, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex social, economic, and political dimensions, including class conflict and regional autonomy issues that transcended purely religious concerns.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1420 is rich for Ming China and Europe, particularly for elite political and military events. The record is dominated by court chronicles, administrative records, and later compilations. African, American, and Asian sources outside the Ming sphere are underrepresented. The perspective is heavily skewed toward official court narratives and elite concerns. Oral traditions, non-elite perspectives, and the experiences of common people are largely absent from surviving records.

graph_edges.note: The relocation of the court to Beijing coincided with the near-completion of the Forbidden City.
Hussite consolidation of control in 1420 led to continued military victories in 1421.
The return of the sixth voyage in 1420 led to the preparation and departure of the seventh voyage in 1421.
The aftermath of the Council of Constance's decisions contributed to the escalation of the Hussite Wars.
